summary: Microsoft directly documents the AppModelUnlock registry method for enabling Windows Developer Mode, and the current app writes that same documented value. This record is now validated with machine-checkable proof.

setting.name: Windows Developer Mode
setting.casual_explanation: Developer Mode turns on Windows support for building, sideloading, and debugging apps. Most casual users do not need it, but developers often do.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable it when they want to install apps directly from an IDE, sideload development builds, or use Windows development features on the local machine.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Developer Mode is useful on development machines, but it is not a normal everyday setting and should usually stay off on general-purpose systems.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft Learn documents a direct Regedit method that creates HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock\AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense = 1 to enable Developer Mode. Microsoft also documents the managed policy counterpart under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Appx with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 in AppxPackageManager.admx. AppxPackageManager.adml says enabling the feature allows development of packaged Microsoft Store apps and installing them directly from an IDE, and notes it works together with the 'Allow all trusted apps to install' policy.

allowed_values.label: Not explicitly enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Developer Mode is not explicitly enabled through the direct AppModelUnlock registry method.

allowed_values.label: Developer Mode enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Developer Mode is enabled so packaged Microsoft Store apps can be developed and installed directly from an IDE.

targets.notes: Microsoft Learn documents this direct registry path as a supported way to enable Developer Mode. Managed environments can also use the Appx policy counterpart.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense = 1 under the same AppModelUnlock path that Microsoft documents in the Regedit method for enabling Developer Mode.

windows_defaults.label: Windows default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows devices where Developer Mode has not been explicitly enabled through the direct AppModelUnlock method

states.rationale: Leave the direct Developer Mode registry value unset unless the machine is intentionally prepared for app development.

windows_defaults.notes: Microsoft documents creating the value to enable Developer Mode. Managed deployments can alternatively use the Appx policy key.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Workstations not used for app development
avoid_for: Developers who need local app sideloading or IDE-driven app deployment

states.rationale: Keep Developer Mode off unless the system is intentionally used for development.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want a normal Windows baseline without development-only features enabled.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You actively build, sideload, or debug apps on this machine.
tradeoffs: The system keeps its normal non-developer baseline.
Direct IDE-based packaged app installation is not enabled through this registry method.

recommended_profiles.label: Developer Mode enabled
avoid_for: Locked-down enterprise endpoints without a development need

states.rationale: Enable the Microsoft-documented AppModelUnlock value for Developer Mode.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You need Windows ready for app development, sideloading, or installing directly from an IDE.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You do not use development features and want to keep the machine closer to the default consumer baseline.
tradeoffs: This setting is useful only for development workflows and adds no benefit for most casual users.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Enable your device for development
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents a Regedit method that creates HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock\AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense = 1 to enable Developer Mode.

evidence.title: Procmon capture - Developer settings search reads AppModelUnlock baseline
evidence.summary: Guest-side Procmon capture showed SearchProtocolHost.exe querying HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock\AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense and receiving NAME NOT FOUND while the developer settings page was open. The same capture also confirmed the LongPathsEnabled read path on this build.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppxPackageManager.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the managed policy counterpart under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Appx with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 for AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppxPackageManager.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the setting allows development of packaged Microsoft Store apps and installing them directly from an IDE, and notes it works together with the 'Allow all trusted apps to install' policy.

decision.why: The official Microsoft page explicitly documents the same AppModelUnlock path and value name that the app writes, so this record now passes the proof gate.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: To enable developer mode, set the values of this DWORD to 1: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock\AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense
validation_proof.notes: The official Microsoft page explicitly names the exact registry path and value used by the app. A guest-side Procmon capture also observed SearchProtocolHost.exe querying the AppModelUnlock key and receiving NAME NOT FOUND for AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense while the developer settings page was open, which matches the missing/default baseline on this build.
